title: Power of 3
description: Check if number is power of 3

approaches.name: Brute Force O(log n)
approaches.summary: Repeatedly divide the number by 3 until it becomes 1 or a non-multiple of 3. Use it for clarity and simplicity when handling small inputs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Check if Number is Power of 3 Using Brute Force Division
ladders.desc: Complete solution that repeatedly divides the number by 3 until reaching 1 or a non-multiple of 3.
ladders.explanation: This brute force approach solves the problem by leveraging the mathematical property that a power of 3 can be reduced to 1 through repeated division by 3. The intuition is simple: if n is a power of 3, then n = 3^k for some integer k ≥ 0. By repeatedly dividing n by 3, we should eventually reach 1 if it's a power of 3. If at any point n is not divisible by 3 (n % 3 != 0) and n != 1, then it's not a power of 3. The algorithm handles edge cases: n must be positive (since 3^k > 0 for all k), and n=1 is a power of 3 (3^0 = 1). The step-by-step algorithm: 1) If n ≤ 0, return False. 2) While n > 1: a) If n % 3 != 0, return False. b) n = n // 3. 3) Return True. Time complexity is O(log₃ n) which simplifies to O(log n) since we divide by 3 each iteration. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete solution using the division subroutine. Common mistakes include: forgetting to handle n=1 (should return True), not checking for n ≤ 0, and infinite loops when n=0. Real code pattern: def isPowerOfThree(n): if n <= 0: return False; while n > 1: if n % 3 != 0: return False; n //= 3; return True.

examples.trace: n=10 → n>1 → 10%3=1 ≠0 → return False

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Repeated Division by 3 Until Condition Met
ladders.desc: Implement the division loop that reduces n by dividing by 3 while checking divisibility.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine forms the core of the brute force approach. The intuition is to transform the problem into a process of repeatedly applying the same operation (division by 3) until a base case is reached. For a number to be a power of 3, it must be divisible by 3 at every step until it becomes 1. The algorithm: while n > 1: if n % 3 != 0: return False; n = n // 3. This loop continues until n becomes 1 (success) or fails divisibility check. Time complexity is O(log₃ n) as each iteration reduces n by a factor of 3. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L0 by being the main computational part of the full solution, and to L2 as it implements the core logic of division. Common mistakes include: not updating n correctly (must use integer division), and off-by-one errors in the loop condition. Real code pattern: while n > 1: if n % 3 != 0: return False; n //= 3.

examples.trace: n=10 → n>1 → 10%3=1 ≠0 → return False

ladders.title: Core Logic — Divisibility Check and Integer Division
ladders.desc: Check if a number is divisible by 3 and perform integer division by 3.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the fundamental operations that drive the algorithm. The intuition is that powers of 3 must be exactly divisible by 3 at each reduction step. The core logic involves two operations: 1) Checking divisibility using modulo operator (n % 3 == 0). 2) Performing integer division (n // 3) to reduce the number. These operations work together: if n is divisible by 3, we divide it to get closer to 1; if not, we know it's not a power of 3. The step-by-step process for a single iteration: if n % 3 == 0: n = n // 3; else: fail. Time complexity per operation is O(1). Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L1 as the operations performed in each loop iteration, and to L3 as it builds on basic arithmetic. Common mistakes include: using floating-point division instead of integer division, and misunderstanding modulo for negative numbers. Real code pattern: if n % 3 == 0: n //= 3.

examples.trace: n=9 → 9%3=0 → divisible → n=9//3=3 → now n=3

ladders.title: Building Block — While Loop with Condition
ladders.desc: Use a while loop to repeatedly perform operations until a condition is met.
ladders.explanation: This level introduces the control flow structure needed for the algorithm. The intuition is that we need to repeat the division operation multiple times until n becomes 1, which requires a loop. A while loop continues executing as long as a condition is true. In our case, we want to keep dividing while n > 1. The algorithm: while n > 1: perform division. This ensures we stop when n reaches 1 (since 1 is our target for powers of 3). The step-by-step: initialize; check condition; if true, execute body; update; repeat. Time complexity depends on number of iterations. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L2 as it provides the structure to repeatedly apply divisibility checks, and to L4 as it uses comparison operations. Common mistakes include: infinite loops (if n doesn't change in loop), and incorrect condition (using n >= 1 would cause extra iteration). Real code pattern: while n > 1: # operations.

examples.trace: n=27 → while 27>1: true → divide → n=9 → while 9>1: true → divide → n=3 → while 3>1: true → divide → n=1 → while 1>1: false → exit
n=4 → while 4>1: true → check divisibility → 4%3=1 ≠0 → return False (loop ends early)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Integer Division and Modulo
ladders.desc: Perform integer division (//) and modulo (%) operations on integers.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the fundamental arithmetic operations required. The intuition is that we need two specific operations: integer division gives the quotient without remainder, and modulo gives the remainder. For example, 10 // 3 = 3 (integer quotient), and 10 % 3 = 1 (remainder). These operations are crucial because: 1) Modulo tells us if a number is divisible by 3 (remainder 0). 2) Integer division reduces the number by factor of 3. The step-by-step for an example: n=10; n%3=1 (not divisible); n//3=3 (but we only use this if divisible). Time complexity is O(1). Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L3 as these operations are used in the loop body, and to L5 as they build on basic arithmetic concepts. Common mistakes include: confusing / (float division) with // (integer division), and not understanding negative modulo results. Real code pattern: quotient = n // 3; remainder = n % 3.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Basic Arithmetic with Integers
ladders.desc: Understand and perform basic arithmetic operations: multiplication, division, and comparison.
ladders.explanation: This foundational level ensures understanding of basic math concepts. The intuition is that checking for powers of 3 relies on arithmetic: a power of 3 means repeated multiplication of 3 (e.g., 3×3×3=27). To reverse this, we use division. Key concepts: 1) Integers are whole numbers. 2) Multiplication (3×3=9). 3) Division as inverse of multiplication (9÷3=3). 4) Comparison (>, =, <). For example, to check if 9 is power of 3: 9÷3=3, then 3÷3=1, so yes. The step-by-step for 9: start with 9; divide by 3 to get 3; divide by 3 to get 1; since we reached 1, it's a power of 3. Time and space complexity are not applicable here as it's conceptual. This connects to L4 as it provides the basis for division operations. Common mistakes include: not understanding integer division, and confusion with fractions. Real code pattern: basic arithmetic expressions like 3 * 3 or 9 / 3.

examples.trace: 10 ÷ 3 = 3.33... not a whole number, so no

approaches.name: Logarithmic O(1)
approaches.summary: Compute the logarithm base 3 of the number and check if the result is an integer. Use it for a concise mathematical solution, but be cautious of floating-point precision issues.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Check if Number is Power of 3 Using Logarithmic Approach
ladders.desc: Complete solution using logarithmic method with floating-point precision handling
ladders.explanation: The logarithmic approach leverages the mathematical property that if n = 3^k, then k = log₃(n) must be an integer. This transforms the problem from checking divisibility patterns to verifying integer nature of a logarithm. The intuition is elegant: powers of 3 follow an exponential pattern, and logarithms are the inverse operation. We compute log₃(n) using the change-of-base formula: log₃(n) = log(n)/log(3). Since we're dealing with floating-point arithmetic, we must handle precision issues carefully. The algorithm: 1) Handle edge cases (n ≤ 0 returns false), 2) Compute log₃(n) using natural or base-10 logs, 3) Check if the result is close to an integer within a small epsilon tolerance, 4) Verify by reconstructing 3^rounded_result to ensure it equals n. Time complexity is O(1) since log computation is constant time. Space complexity is O(1) using only a few variables. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete integer check with precision handling. Common mistakes include: not handling negative/zero inputs, using == instead of tolerance comparison for floating-point, forgetting to verify with power reconstruction, and choosing inappropriate epsilon values. Real code pattern: def isPowerOfThree(n): if n <= 0: return False; log_val = math.log(n) / math.log(3); return abs(log_val - round(log_val)) < 1e-10 and 3**round(log_val) == n.

examples.trace: Step 1: n = 27 > 0, proceed. Step 2: Compute log₃(27) = ln(27)/ln(3) = 3.2958.../1.0986... = 3.0000000000000004. Step 3: Round to nearest integer = 3. Step 4: Difference = |3.0000000000000004 - 3| = 4.44e-16 < 1e-10. Step 5: Verify: 3^3 = 27 equals n. Step 6: Return True.

Step 1: n = 45 > 0, proceed. Step 2: Compute log₃(45) = ln(45)/ln(3) = 3.8066.../1.0986... = 3.464973... Step 3: Round to nearest integer = 3. Step 4: Difference = |3.464973 - 3| = 0.464973 > 1e-10. Step 5: Since difference exceeds tolerance, return False without verification.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Check if Logarithm Result is Integer with Precision Tolerance
ladders.desc: Determine if a floating-point number represents an integer within tolerance
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine solves the core challenge of the logarithmic approach: determining whether a computed logarithm value represents an exact integer despite floating-point imprecision. The intuition is that floating-point calculations introduce tiny errors (e.g., log₃(27) might compute as 3.0000000000000004 instead of exactly 3), so we need a tolerance-based comparison rather than exact equality. The algorithm: 1) Take a floating-point input value, 2) Round it to the nearest integer using proper rounding function, 3) Calculate absolute difference between original and rounded values, 4) Compare difference against a small epsilon (typically 1e-10 for double precision), 5) Return True if difference < epsilon, False otherwise. Time complexity is O(1) for basic arithmetic operations. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L0 by providing the precision-handling mechanism needed for the full solution. Common mistakes include: using fixed epsilon without considering magnitude of numbers, using integer casting instead of proper rounding, and not understanding that floating-point errors accumulate in log calculations. Real code pattern: def isCloseToInteger(x, epsilon=1e-10): rounded = round(x); return abs(x - rounded) < epsilon.

examples.trace: Step 1: x = 3.0000000000000004. Step 2: Round to nearest integer: round(3.0000000000000004) = 3. Step 3: Difference = |3.0000000000000004 - 3| = 4.44e-16. Step 4: Compare with epsilon=1e-10: 4.44e-16 < 1e-10 is True. Step 5: Return True.

Step 1: x = 3.464973520717926. Step 2: Round to nearest integer: round(3.464973520717926) = 3. Step 3: Difference = |3.464973520717926 - 3| = 0.464973520717926. Step 4: Compare with epsilon=1e-10: 0.464973520717926 < 1e-10 is False. Step 5: Return False.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Compute Logarithm Base 3 Using Change-of-Base Formula
ladders.desc: Calculate log₃(n) by transforming to available logarithm functions
ladders.explanation: This core logic implements the mathematical transformation needed to compute logarithms with base 3 when programming languages typically only provide natural log (ln) or base-10 log. The intuition comes from the change-of-base formula: logₐ(b) = logₓ(b)/logₓ(a) for any valid base x. We use this to compute log₃(n) = log(n)/log(3), where log can be natural log or base-10 log. The algorithm: 1) Take positive input n, 2) Compute log(n) using math.log() or math.log10(), 3) Compute log(3) using same function, 4) Divide results: log(n)/log(3), 5) Return the floating-point result. Time complexity is O(1) for constant-time log operations. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L1 by providing the logarithmic value that needs integer checking. Common mistakes include: using integer division instead of float division, applying formula to non-positive numbers (log undefined), and not realizing that natural log and base-10 log give same result after division due to formula consistency. Real code pattern: def logBase3(n): return math.log(n) / math.log(3).

ladders.title: Building Block — Round Floating-Point Number to Nearest Integer
ladders.desc: Convert a float to the closest integer value with proper rounding
ladders.explanation: This building block addresses how to properly round floating-point numbers to integers, which is essential for checking if a logarithmic result represents an integer. The intuition is that we need to handle values exactly midway between integers (like 2.5) consistently and deal with how programming languages handle rounding. The algorithm: 1) Take floating-point input x, 2) Use round() function which implements 'round half to even' (banker's rounding) in many languages, 3) For this problem, we can use simple rounding: floor(x + 0.5) for positive numbers handles standard rounding, 4) Convert result to integer type. Time complexity is O(1). Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L2 by providing the rounding needed after computing the logarithm. Common mistakes include: using int() truncation instead of rounding, not handling negative numbers correctly, and not understanding that round() returns float in some languages. Real code pattern: def roundToInt(x): return int(round(x)) or return int(x + 0.5) for positive x.

examples.trace: Step 1: x = 3.7. Step 2: Using round(3.7) = 4. Step 3: Convert to int: int(4) = 4. Step 4: Return 4.

Step 1: x = 2.5. Step 2: Using round(2.5) with banker's rounding = 2 (since 2 is even). Step 3: Convert to int: int(2) = 2. Step 4: Return 2. Note: Some implementations might return 3 with different rounding.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compute Absolute Difference Between Two Numbers
ladders.desc: Calculate |a - b| to measure distance between values
ladders.explanation: This basic operation computes the absolute difference between two numbers, which is fundamental for comparing floating-point values with tolerance. The intuition is that we need to measure how close two numbers are regardless of which is larger. The algorithm: 1) Take two numerical inputs a and b, 2) Subtract: diff = a - b, 3) If diff < 0, multiply by -1 to make positive (or use absolute value function), 4) Return the positive difference. Time complexity is O(1) for subtraction and conditional. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L3 by providing the difference measurement needed for rounding comparisons. Common mistakes include: not handling negative results properly, using squared difference when absolute is needed, and integer overflow with large numbers. Real code pattern: def absDiff(a, b): return abs(a - b).

examples.trace: Step 1: a = 5, b = 3. Step 2: Compute difference: 5 - 3 = 2. Step 3: Since 2 ≥ 0, absolute value = 2. Step 4: Return 2.
Step 1: a = 3, b = 5. Step 2: Compute difference: 3 - 5 = -2. Step 3: Since -2 < 0, take absolute: |-2| = 2. Step 4: Return 2.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Integer vs Floating-Point Numbers
ladders.desc: Distinguish between exact integers and approximate floating-point representations
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept explains the fundamental difference between integer and floating-point number representations in computers, which is crucial for understanding why we need tolerance comparisons in the logarithmic approach. Integers are exact representations of whole numbers, while floating-point numbers are approximations of real numbers with limited precision. The intuition: computers store floating-point numbers in binary scientific notation with a fixed number of bits, leading to rounding errors in calculations. For example, 0.1 in decimal cannot be represented exactly in binary, similar to how 1/3 cannot be represented exactly in decimal. The algorithm for understanding: 1) Identify integer values (whole numbers without fractional part), 2) Recognize that calculations involving division, logarithms, or irrational numbers produce floating-point results, 3) Understand that two floating-point numbers should be compared with tolerance, not exact equality. This connects to L4 by establishing why we need absolute difference instead of exact comparison. Common mistakes include: expecting exact results from floating-point arithmetic, using == to compare floats, and not understanding that 3.0 and 3 are different types but represent the same mathematical value. Real code pattern: Understanding that 3 == 3.0 is True but type(3) != type(3.0).

examples.trace: Step 1: Value is 3. Step 2: It has no fractional part. Step 3: In programming, isinstance(3, int) returns True. Step 4: Mathematically, 3 ∈ ℤ. Step 5: Therefore, 3 is an integer.

Step 1: First value is 3.0000000000000004 (floating-point). Step 2: Second value is 3 (integer). Step 3: Exact comparison: 3.0000000000000004 == 3 evaluates to False. Step 4: Difference is 4.44e-16. Step 5: With tolerance 1e-10, they are approximately equal.

approaches.name: Precomputed Powers O(1)
approaches.summary: Store all powers of 3 within the integer range (e.g., up to 2^31-1) in a set or list, then check membership. Use it for optimal runtime in constrained environments where memory overhead is acceptable.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(1) due to fixed number of powers (e.g., 20 for 32-bit integers)

ladders.title: Full Problem — Check if Number is Power of 3 Using Precomputed Powers
ladders.desc: Complete solution using precomputed powers of 3 within integer range
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the 'Power of 3' problem by leveraging the mathematical property that there are only a limited number of powers of 3 that fit within standard integer ranges. For 32-bit signed integers (range -2^31 to 2^31-1), there are exactly 20 positive powers of 3 (3^0 to 3^19) that don't overflow. The intuition is simple: instead of repeatedly dividing or using logarithms at runtime, we can precompute all valid powers once and store them in a hash set for O(1) lookup. This transforms the problem from a mathematical computation to a simple membership check. The algorithm works by first generating all powers of 3 up to the maximum integer value, storing them in a set, then checking if the input number exists in this set. Time complexity is O(1) for the check operation since set lookup is constant time, and space complexity is O(1) because we store a fixed number of elements (20 for 32-bit integers). This approach is particularly valuable in constrained environments where memory overhead is acceptable but runtime must be optimal. Connection to real-world applications includes scenarios where this check needs to be performed repeatedly in tight loops or real-time systems. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle negative numbers (powers of 3 are always positive), not considering integer overflow when generating powers, and using lists instead of sets for lookup (which would be O(n)). The real code pattern involves creating a constant set of powers during initialization, then simply checking 'return n in POWER_SET'.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Precomputed powers set contains: {1, 3, 9, 27, 81, 243, 729, 2187, 6561, 19683, 59049, 177147, 531441, 1594323, 4782969, 14348907, 43046721, 129140163, 387420489, 1162261467}
Step 2: Check if 27 exists in the set
Step 3: 27 is found in the set
Step 4: Return true

Step 1: Precomputed powers set contains: {1, 3, 9, 27, 81, 243, 729, 2187, 6561, 19683, 59049, 177147, 531441, 1594323, 4782969, 14348907, 43046721, 129140163, 387420489, 1162261467}
Step 2: Check if 28 exists in the set
Step 3: 28 is not found in the set
Step 4: Return false

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Generate All Powers of 3 Within Integer Range
ladders.desc: Create a set containing all powers of 3 that fit in 32-bit signed integers
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the precomputation approach. The intuition is that we need to generate every possible power of 3 that doesn't cause integer overflow, as these are the only numbers that could possibly be valid inputs. For 32-bit signed integers, the maximum value is 2,147,483,647. We start with 3^0 = 1 and repeatedly multiply by 3 until we either exceed the maximum integer value or reach a predetermined limit. The algorithm uses a while loop that multiplies the current value by 3 in each iteration, checking for overflow by ensuring the result doesn't exceed INTEGER_MAX / 3. The generated powers are stored in a hash set for optimal lookup. Time complexity is O(k) where k is the number of powers generated (20 for 32-bit integers), which is effectively O(1). Space complexity is O(k) for storing the set. This feeds into the parent problem by providing the precomputed data structure that enables O(1) checking. Common mistakes include not starting with 3^0 = 1 (which is a valid power of 3), not handling the overflow check correctly, and generating too many or too few powers. The real code pattern involves initializing an empty set, starting with power = 1, and while power <= MAX_INT: add power to set, power *= 3, with overflow protection.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Set Membership Check for Constant Time Lookup
ladders.desc: Check if an element exists in a precomputed hash set
ladders.explanation: This core logic implements the actual check operation using hash set membership. The intuition is that hash sets provide average O(1) time complexity for lookup operations by using hash functions to map elements to specific indices. When we have a precomputed set of all valid powers of 3, checking if a number is a power of 3 becomes a simple 'is element in set' operation. The algorithm takes the input number and uses the 'in' operator (or equivalent contains() method) to check membership in the constant set. The hash set works by computing a hash code for the input, using it to find the appropriate bucket, then checking if the element exists in that bucket. Time complexity is O(1) on average for hash set lookup. Space complexity is O(1) since the set size is fixed. This feeds into level 1 by being the operation performed after generating the set. Common mistakes include using a list instead of a set (which would be O(n) lookup), not considering that sets don't maintain order, and forgetting that sets cannot contain duplicates. The real code pattern is simply 'return n in power_set' where power_set is the precomputed constant set.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Input number is 9
Step 2: Compute hash of 9 (simplified: hash(9) = 9)
Step 3: Use hash to find bucket index (simplified: bucket = 9 % 4 = 1)
Step 4: Check bucket 1 which contains [9]
Step 5: 9 equals 9, found match
Step 6: Return true

Step 1: Input number is 5
Step 2: Compute hash of 5 (simplified: hash(5) = 5)
Step 3: Use hash to find bucket index (simplified: bucket = 5 % 4 = 1)
Step 4: Check bucket 1 which contains [9]
Step 5: 5 does not equal 9, no match
Step 6: Return false

ladders.title: Building Block — Integer Overflow Prevention in Multiplication
ladders.desc: Safely multiply integers while checking for overflow
ladders.explanation: This building block addresses the critical issue of integer overflow when generating powers of 3. The intuition is that when we repeatedly multiply by 3, the result may exceed the maximum representable integer value, causing undefined behavior or incorrect results. We need to detect this before it happens. The algorithm checks before each multiplication whether the current value exceeds MAX_INT / 3. If it does, we stop multiplying to avoid overflow. This works because if current > MAX_INT / 3, then current * 3 > MAX_INT. We use integer division for the check. Time complexity is O(1) per check. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into level 2 by ensuring the power generation subroutine doesn't produce invalid values. Common mistakes include checking after multiplication (too late), using floating point division instead of integer division, and not considering negative numbers (though powers of 3 are always positive). The real code pattern is: 'if current > MAX_INT // 3: break' before multiplying.

examples.trace:
Step 1: MAX_INT = 500
Step 2: MAX_INT // 3 = 500 // 3 = 166
Step 3: current = 100
Step 4: Check if current > MAX_INT // 3: 100 > 166? No
Step 5: Therefore safe to multiply: 100 * 3 = 300
Step 6: 300 <= 500, no overflow

Step 1: MAX_INT = 500
Step 2: MAX_INT // 3 = 500 // 3 = 166
Step 3: current = 200
Step 4: Check if current > MAX_INT // 3: 200 > 166? Yes
Step 5: Therefore unsafe to multiply: 200 * 3 = 600 would exceed 500
Step 6: Stop to prevent overflow

ladders.title: Basic Operation — While Loop with Accumulating Multiplication
ladders.desc: Use a while loop to repeatedly multiply a number by a constant
ladders.explanation: This basic operation demonstrates how to use a while loop to generate a sequence by repeatedly multiplying by a constant factor. The intuition is that we want to create geometric progression: start with 1, then 1×3=3, then 3×3=9, etc. The algorithm initializes a variable, then uses a while loop with a condition (usually based on not exceeding a limit) to repeatedly multiply the variable by the constant. Each iteration updates the variable. Time complexity is O(n) where n is the number of iterations. Space complexity is O(1) for the variables. This feeds into level 3 by providing the loop structure for generating powers. Common mistakes include infinite loops (forgetting to update the variable), off-by-one errors in the condition, and using the wrong operator (e.g., addition instead of multiplication). The real code pattern is: 'power = 1; while power <= limit: # do something with power; power *= 3'.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize power = 1
Step 2: Check while condition: 1 <= 20? Yes
Step 3: Process power = 1
Step 4: Multiply power by 2: power = 2
Step 5: Check while condition: 2 <= 20? Yes
Step 6: Process power = 2
Step 7: Multiply power by 2: power = 4
Step 8: Check while condition: 4 <= 20? Yes
Step 9: Process power = 4
Step 10: Multiply power by 2: power = 8
Step 11: Check while condition: 8 <= 20? Yes
Step 12: Process power = 8
Step 13: Multiply power by 2: power = 16
Step 14: Check while condition: 16 <= 20? Yes
Step 15: Process power = 16
Step 16: Multiply power by 2: power = 32
Step 17: Check while condition: 32 <= 20? No, stop
Sequence generated: 1, 2, 4, 8, 16

Step 1: Initialize power = 1
Step 2: Check while condition: 1 <= 100? Yes
Step 3: Process power = 1
Step 4: Multiply power by 5: power = 5
Step 5: Check while condition: 5 <= 100? Yes
Step 6: Process power = 5
Step 7: Multiply power by 5: power = 25
Step 8: Check while condition: 25 <= 100? Yes
Step 9: Process power = 25
Step 10: Multiply power by 5: power = 125
Step 11: Check while condition: 125 <= 100? No, stop
Sequence generated: 1, 5, 25

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Integer Multiplication and Comparison
ladders.desc: Multiply integers and compare them with other values
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers the most basic operations needed: integer multiplication and comparison. The intuition is that computers perform arithmetic operations on numbers, and we need to understand how to multiply two integers and compare the result to another value. Multiplication is repeated addition: a × b means adding a to itself b times. Comparison checks if one number is less than, greater than, or equal to another. The algorithm for multiplication is simply using the * operator. For comparison, we use operators like <, >, <=, >=, ==. Time complexity is O(1) for both operations on fixed-size integers. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into level 4 by providing the operations used in the while loop. Common mistakes include confusing multiplication with exponentiation, not understanding integer division vs float division, and using assignment (=) instead of comparison (==). The real code pattern includes statements like 'result = a * b' and 'if result < limit:'.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Multiply 4 by 3: 4 * 3 = 12
Step 2: Compare 12 < 15
Step 3: 12 is less than 15
Step 4: Therefore the comparison is true

Step 1: Multiply 5 by 4: 5 * 4 = 20
Step 2: Compare 20 > 25
Step 3: 20 is not greater than 25
Step 4: Therefore the comparison is false

approaches.name: Integer Limits Division O(1)
approaches.summary: Find the largest power of 3 within the integer range (e.g., 3^19 for 32-bit), then check if the input divides this number evenly. Use it for an efficient, division-based method without precomputation overhead.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Power of 3 Check Using Integer Limits Division
ladders.desc: Complete solution to determine if a given integer is a power of 3 using the largest power of 3 within integer range.
ladders.explanation: This approach leverages the mathematical property that if n is a power of 3, it must divide the largest power of 3 that fits within the integer range (e.g., 3^19 = 1162261467 for 32-bit signed integers). The intuition is based on number theory: for prime p, if n = p^k, then n divides p^max where max is the maximum exponent such that p^max fits in the integer range, provided k ≤ max. This works because powers of a prime have unique prime factorization, and any smaller power will be a divisor of a larger power of the same base. The algorithm first handles edge cases: n must be positive (since powers of 3 are positive), and n=0 returns false. Then, it computes the largest power of 3 (max_power) that fits in the integer range—for 32-bit signed integers, this is 3^19 = 1162261467, as 3^20 exceeds 2^31-1. The check is simply: return n > 0 and max_power % n == 0. Time complexity is O(1) because it involves constant-time arithmetic operations. Space complexity is O(1) as only a few variables are used. This method avoids loops or recursion, making it highly efficient. Connection to L1: This full solution uses the key subroutine of computing the largest power of 3. Common mistakes include not checking for negative numbers or zero, using the wrong max power for the integer type, or misunderstanding the division condition. Ensure the integer type matches the problem constraints (e.g., 32-bit signed integers). Real code pattern: def isPowerOfThree(n): max_power = 1162261467; return n > 0 and max_power % n == 0.

examples.trace: Step 1: Input n = 27. Step 2: Check n > 0? 27 > 0 → True. Step 3: max_power = 1162261467 (precomputed as 3^19). Step 4: Compute max_power % n = 1162261467 % 27. Calculate: 27 * 43046721 = 1162261467, remainder = 0. Step 5: Condition max_power % n == 0 is True. Step 6: Return True.

Step 1: Input n = 10. Step 2: Check n > 0? 10 > 0 → True. Step 3: max_power = 1162261467. Step 4: Compute max_power % n = 1162261467 % 10. Calculate: 10 * 116226146 = 1162261460, remainder = 7. Step 5: Condition max_power % n == 0 is False (7 != 0). Step 6: Return False.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compute Largest Power of 3 in Integer Range
ladders.desc: Determine the maximum power of 3 that fits within a given integer range (e.g., 32-bit signed integers).
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine finds the largest integer of the form 3^k that does not exceed the maximum value of the integer type (e.g., 2^31-1 for 32-bit signed integers). The intuition is to iteratively multiply by 3 until the next multiplication would overflow or exceed the limit, ensuring we stay within bounds. This is crucial for the main problem because it provides the constant max_power used in the division check. The algorithm starts with power = 1 (since 3^0 = 1) and repeatedly multiplies by 3 while power * 3 ≤ max_limit. For 32-bit signed integers, max_limit = 2147483647. The loop continues until the condition fails, and the last valid power is returned. Time complexity is O(log_3(max_limit)), which is O(1) since max_limit is constant (e.g., about 19 iterations for 32-bit). Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L0: This computed value is used directly in the full solution. Connection to L2: This relies on the core logic of exponentiation and overflow prevention. Common mistakes include starting with power = 0 (incorrect), not using the correct integer limit, or infinite loops if the condition is wrong. Always verify the limit based on the problem's integer constraints. Real code pattern: def largestPowerOfThree(limit): power = 1; while power * 3 <= limit: power *= 3; return power.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize power = 1. Step 2: Check power * 3 <= 100? 1*3=3 <= 100 → True, set power = 3. Step 3: Check 3*3=9 <= 100 → True, set power = 9. Step 4: Check 9*3=27 <= 100 → True, set power = 27. Step 5: Check 27*3=81 <= 100 → True, set power = 81. Step 6: Check 81*3=243 <= 100 → False. Step 7: Return power = 81.
Step 1: Initialize power = 1. Step 2: Check 1*3=3 <= 10 → True, set power = 3. Step 3: Check 3*3=9 <= 10 → True, set power = 9. Step 4: Check 9*3=27 <= 10 → False. Step 5: Return power = 9.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Divisibility Check for Powers of a Prime
ladders.desc: Use divisibility to test if a number is a power of a prime by checking if it divides the largest power of that prime within a range.
ladders.explanation: This core logic is based on the mathematical principle that for a prime number p, if n is a power of p (i.e., n = p^k), then n divides p^m for any m ≥ k. Specifically, if we take the largest power of p that fits in the integer range (p^max), then n divides p^max if and only if n is a power of p with exponent k ≤ max. The intuition comes from prime factorization: p^max has only p as its prime factors, so any divisor must also be a power of p. This avoids needing to compute logarithms or use loops, providing an O(1) check. The algorithm involves: given n and a precomputed max_power = p^max, check if n > 0 and max_power % n == 0. Time and space complexity are O(1). Connection to L1: This logic uses the largest power computed in the sub-routine. Connection to L3: It relies on understanding modulo operation and divisibility. Common mistakes include applying this to non-prime bases (which may not work due to multiple prime factors), forgetting to check n > 0, or using the wrong max_power. Ensure p is prime (3 is prime). Real code pattern: def isPowerOfPrime(n, max_power): return n > 0 and max_power % n == 0.

examples.trace: Step 1: Input n = 9, max_power = 81. Step 2: Check n > 0? 9 > 0 → True. Step 3: Compute max_power % n = 81 % 9. Calculate: 9 * 9 = 81, remainder = 0. Step 4: Condition max_power % n == 0 is True. Step 5: Return True.

Step 1: Input n = 10, max_power = 81. Step 2: Check n > 0? 10 > 0 → True. Step 3: Compute max_power % n = 81 % 10. Calculate: 10 * 8 = 80, remainder = 1. Step 4: Condition max_power % n == 0 is False (1 != 0). Step 5: Return False.

ladders.title: Building Block — Modulo Operation and Divisibility
ladders.desc: Understand and apply the modulo operator to check if one number divides another evenly.
ladders.explanation: The modulo operation (%) returns the remainder when one number is divided by another. Divisibility means that when a divides b, b % a == 0. This is a fundamental concept in number theory and is essential for the power-of-3 check. The intuition is that if n divides max_power without a remainder, then n is a factor of max_power. In the context of powers of 3, this helps identify if n is composed only of the prime factor 3. The algorithm for checking divisibility: given two integers a and b, compute b % a; if the result is 0, then a divides b. Time and space complexity are O(1). Connection to L2: This operation is used in the core logic to test divisibility. Connection to L4: It involves basic arithmetic operations. Common mistakes include confusing dividend and divisor (e.g., using n % max_power instead of max_power % n), not handling division by zero (which causes an error), or misunderstanding negative numbers (modulo with negatives can be language-dependent; in this context, we ensure positive inputs). Always use positive numbers to avoid issues. Real code pattern: def divides(a, b): return b % a == 0 if a != 0 else False (with error handling).

examples.trace: Step 1: Input a = 3, b = 9. Step 2: Compute b % a = 9 % 3. Step 3: Perform division: 9 ÷ 3 = 3 remainder 0. Step 4: Check if remainder == 0 → True. Step 5: Return True (3 divides 9).

Step 1: Input a = 4, b = 9. Step 2: Compute b % a = 9 % 4. Step 3: Perform division: 9 ÷ 4 = 2 remainder 1. Step 4: Check if remainder == 0 → False (1 != 0). Step 5: Return False (4 does not divide 9).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Integer Multiplication and Comparison
ladders.desc: Perform multiplication of integers and compare results to a limit, focusing on overflow prevention.
ladders.explanation: This involves multiplying an integer by a constant (e.g., 3) and checking if the result is within a specified limit (e.g., maximum integer value). The intuition is to safely compute powers without causing overflow, which is critical when finding the largest power of 3 in an integer range. The algorithm: given a current value 'power' and a limit 'max_limit', compute 'power * 3' and compare it to 'max_limit'. If 'power * 3 <= max_limit', then the multiplication is safe, and we update 'power'. Otherwise, stop. This prevents integer overflow, which can lead to incorrect results or errors. Time and space complexity are O(1) per operation. Connection to L3: This operation is used in the building block for iterative exponentiation. Connection to L5: It relies on basic arithmetic concepts. Common mistakes include not using the correct comparison (e.g., using < instead of <=, which might exclude the limit), ignoring overflow in languages without automatic checks, or using floating-point numbers which can introduce precision issues. Always use integer arithmetic and ensure the multiplication does not exceed the limit. Real code pattern: if power * 3 <= limit: power = power * 3.

examples.trace: Step 1: Input power = 9, limit = 30. Step 2: Compute power * 3 = 9 * 3 = 27. Step 3: Check if 27 <= 30 → True. Step 4: Update power = 27. Step 5: Return power = 27.
Step 1: Input power = 27, limit = 30. Step 2: Compute power * 3 = 27 * 3 = 81. Step 3: Check if 81 <= 30 → False. Step 4: Do not update power. Step 5: Return power = 27.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Positive Integers and Basic Arithmetic
ladders.desc: Understand positive integers and perform basic arithmetic operations like multiplication and comparison.
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers the basics of positive integers (whole numbers greater than zero) and simple arithmetic operations. Positive integers are essential because powers of 3 are always positive (e.g., 3^0=1, 3^1=3, etc.). The intuition is that mathematical properties like divisibility and exponentiation are defined for positive integers in this context. Basic operations include multiplication (e.g., 3 * 3 = 9) and comparison (e.g., checking if 9 <= 10). These are building blocks for more advanced algorithms. The algorithm for this level is straightforward: given two positive integers a and b, compute a * b and compare the result to a limit c. For example, to check if multiplying by 3 stays within a limit. Time and space complexity are O(1). Connection to L4: This foundation is used in the basic operation for multiplication and comparison. Common mistakes include confusing positive with non-negative (including zero), incorrect multiplication facts, or misapplying comparison operators. Practice with small numbers to build confidence. Real code pattern: result = a * b; if result <= c: print('Within limit').

examples.trace: Step 1: Input a = 3, b = 4, limit = 15. Step 2: Compute a * b = 3 * 4 = 12. Step 3: Check if 12 <= 15 → True. Step 4: Output 'Within limit'.
Step 1: Input a = 5, b = 5, limit = 20. Step 2: Compute a * b = 5 * 5 = 25. Step 3: Check if 25 <= 20 → False. Step 4: Output 'Exceeds limit'.
